iterations.strategy: Root cause (Category A + C): Two compounding issues in iter_0. First, long double accumulators force x87 FPU usage; with 30 accumulators (5 per slot × 6 slots) vs only 8 x87 registers, the compiler spills 22 accumulators to stack every iteration, generating ~352 extra bytes of memory traffic per row on top of the 38 bytes of column data — this alone roughly doubles effective bandwidth pressure. Switching to double eliminates the spilling, fits 6 slots comfortably in YMM/ZMM registers, and enables AVX-512 vectorization of the arithmetic. Second, the current code never loads zone_max from the zone map, so ALL passing blocks go through the scalar slow path with a per-row date branch; adding the three-way classification (skip/fast-path/slow-path) allows ~588 of 600 blocks to use a branch-free #pragma omp simd inner loop. Third, the ~34ms unaccounted gap in iter_0 is OMP thread pool creation (64 OS threads on first omp_get_max_threads() call, outside any measured phase); firing a dummy parallel region inside data_loading absorbs this cost into a phase that already waits for mmap/madvise syscalls. Combined, these changes target eliminating the x87 spill overhead and enabling SIMD vectorization, with the goal of bringing main_scan from 40ms closer to the memory-bandwidth limit (~20ms at ~55% DRAM efficiency on this NUMA system).

Root cause (Category D — Parallelism tuning): iter_1 places a dummy OMP warmup region outside any measured phase, leaving ~34ms of dead time (64 OS thread creations via pthread_create, ~0.5ms each) that adds directly to wall-clock total. In OMP's fork-join model all threads must be created and reach the parallel barrier before any work starts, so this overhead is unavoidable. The fix is to replace OMP with std::thread + atomic work-stealing: the main thread starts scanning blocks at t=0 using std::atomic<int> next_block; 7 worker threads are created asynchronously and immediately join the same work-stealing loop. Thread creation time (~3.5ms for 7 threads) overlaps with DRAM reads on the main thread instead of being dead time. Thread count is reduced from 64 to 8: 8 threads easily saturate the observed 60 GB/s DRAM bandwidth (sequential reads, hardware prefetcher sustains ~8-12 GB/s per core), so scan time stays at ~34ms while the thread-creation overhead drops from ~34ms serial to ~3.5ms overlapped. Estimated total: ~35-36ms vs 68ms. Accumulator precision is unchanged from iter_1: long double for sum_disc_price and sum_charge to preserve correctness of large accumulated sums; double for sum_qty, sum_base_price, sum_disc. Zone-map three-way classification and fast-path branch-free loop are preserved unchanged.
